sections.title: Basic Numbers 1–10
sections.explanation:
The basic Chinese numbers 1–10 are: 一 (yī) 1, 二 (èr) 2, 三 (sān) 3, 四 (sì) 4, 五 (wǔ) 5, 六 (liù) 6, 七 (qī) 7, 八 (bā) 8, 九 (jiǔ) 9, 十 (shí) 10. Zero is 零 (líng). Numbers 11–19 are formed by saying 十 plus the unit: 十一 (11), 十二 (12), etc. Multiples of ten use the multiplier before 十: 二十 (20), 三十 (30). Compound numbers combine both: 二十五 (25) = 二十 + 五.

Chinese has a unique system of one-handed gestures for numbers 1–10 that lets people show any number on a single hand. 1–5 use the same finger-counting as in the West, but 6–10 are distinct hand shapes: 6 is thumb and pinky extended (like a phone gesture), 7 is thumb and first two fingers pinched together, 8 is thumb and index finger extended in an L-shape, 9 is a hooked index finger, and 10 is either a closed fist or crossed index fingers forming a 十 shape.
sections.tip: 一 changes tone in context: before a 4th-tone syllable it becomes yí (2nd tone), and before 1st/2nd/3rd-tone syllables it becomes yì (4th tone). When counting alone, say yī.

examples.zh: 一二三四五六七八九十
examples.pinyin: yī èr sān sì wǔ liù qī bā jiǔ shí
examples.en: One two three four five six seven eight nine ten
examples.highlight: 一二三四五六七八九十

examples.en: She is 25 years old this year.

examples.pinyin: Qǐng gěi wǒ liù gè píngguǒ.

exercises.prompt: How do you say the number 17 in Chinese?
exercises.explanation: 17 is 十七 (shíqī) — ten plus seven. 七十 would be 70.
三十八 (sānshíbā) = 38. Combine the tens (三十) with the unit (八).

exercises.prompt: Which hand gesture represents the number 6 in the Chinese one-hand system?
options: An L-shape with thumb and index finger
All five fingers open plus a wave
exercises.explanation: The number 6 is shown by extending the thumb and pinky while closing the other three fingers — it looks like a telephone gesture.

sections.title: Larger Numbers
sections.explanation:
Chinese groups large numbers by ten-thousands (万 wàn = 10,000) rather than by thousands as in Western languages. The key units are: 百 (bǎi) = 100, 千 (qiān) = 1,000, 万 (wàn) = 10,000, 亿 (yì) = 100,000,000 (one hundred million). So one million is 一百万 (yī bǎi wàn, literally 'one hundred ten-thousands'), and one billion is 十亿 (shí yì, literally 'ten hundred-millions').

When there is a zero in the middle of a number, you must say 零 (líng) once to mark the gap, but you never repeat it for consecutive zeros. For example: 305 = 三百零五, 1,020 = 一千零二十, but 1,005 = 一千零五 (one 零 covers both missing tens and hundreds). Trailing zeros are never read aloud: 400 = 四百 (not 四百零零).
sections.tip: To convert a Western number to the 万 system: split at the ten-thousands place. 250,000 = 25 万 = 二十五万. 13,000,000 = 1,300 万 = 一千三百万.

examples.pinyin: Zhè běn shū sānbǎi wǔshí kuài.
Nàge chéngshì yǒu bābǎi wàn rén.
Fángjiān hào shì sān líng wǔ.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 10,000 in Chinese?
exercises.explanation: Chinese uses 万 (wàn) for 10,000 as a base unit. You say 一万 (yī wàn), not 十千.
50,000 = 五万 (wǔ wàn). Divide by 10,000 to get the number before 万.

exercises.prompt: How do you say the number 1,008 in Chinese?
exercises.explanation: 1,008 = 一千零八. Use a single 零 to bridge the gap (the missing hundreds and tens), never multiple 零 in a row.

sections.title: Ordinal Numbers and Dates
sections.explanation:
Ordinal numbers are formed by placing 第 (dì) before the cardinal number: 第一 (dìyī) = first, 第二 (dì'èr) = second, 第三 (dìsān) = third, and so on. This pattern works for any number: 第十五 = fifteenth, 第一百 = one hundredth.

Chinese dates go from largest to smallest unit: 年 (nián, year) → 月 (yuè, month) → 日/号 (rì/hào, day). The year is read digit by digit: 2024年 = 二零二四年 (èr líng èr sì nián). Months are simply numbered 1–12: 一月 (January) through 十二月 (December). Days use 日 in formal writing and 号 (hào) in speech: 三月五号 = March 5th.

Days of the week use 星期 (xīngqī) plus a number: 星期一 (Monday) through 星期六 (Saturday). Sunday breaks the pattern: 星期日 or 星期天 (xīngqītiān).
sections.tip: Years are read digit by digit — 1998年 is 一九九八年, not 一千九百九十八年. This is different from how you read other large numbers!

examples.zh: 今天是二零二四年三月五号。
examples.pinyin: Jīntiān shì èr líng èr sì nián sān yuè wǔ hào.

examples.en: I have Chinese class on Wednesday.

examples.pinyin: Tā de shēngrì shì shí yuè yī hào.

exercises.prompt: March 15th in Chinese (spoken style): 三月___号
exercises.explanation: March 15th = 三月十五号. The day uses the cardinal number before 号.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Thursday' in Chinese?
exercises.explanation: Thursday = 星期四 (xīngqīsì). The days Monday–Saturday use 一 through 六.

exercises.prompt: How is the year 2008 read in Chinese?
exercises.explanation: Years are read digit by digit: 2008 = 二零零八年 (èr líng líng bā nián). Each digit is spoken individually.

sections.title: Money and Phone Numbers
sections.explanation:
Chinese currency (人民币 Rénmínbì, RMB) has three units: 元/块 (yuán/kuài) for the main unit (like dollars), 角/毛 (jiǎo/máo) for tenths, and 分 (fēn) for hundredths. In everyday speech, 块 and 毛 are used; 元 and 角 appear on price tags and formal contexts. For example, ¥3.50 = 三块五毛 or simply 三块五 (the 毛 can be dropped when there are no 分). When there are 分: ¥8.25 = 八块二毛五分 or 八块二毛五.

Important: use 两 (liǎng) instead of 二 (èr) before measure words and units like 块: 两块钱 (two yuan), not 二块钱. However, 二 is used in larger compound numbers: 二十块 (20 yuan), 十二块 (12 yuan), and 二百块 (200 yuan) are all fine.

Phone numbers are read digit by digit, with 一 (yāo, using a special reading to avoid confusion with 七 qī) for the digit 1: the number 110 (emergency) = 幺幺零 (yāo yāo líng). Mobile numbers like 138-1234-5678 are read as individual digits, often grouped rhythmically.
sections.tip: 二 vs 两: use 两 before measure words (两个, 两块, 两杯) and 二 in sequential/mathematical contexts (第二, 二十, 一二三). In phone numbers, 1 is read as 幺 (yāo), not 一 (yī).

examples.pinyin: Zhège duōshao qián? Liǎng kuài wǔ.

examples.zh: 我的电话号码是幺三八幺二三四五六七八。
examples.pinyin: Wǒ de diànhuà hàomǎ shì yāo sān bā yāo èr sān sì wǔ liù qī bā.
examples.highlight: 幺三八幺二三四五六七八

examples.pinyin: Qǐng gěi wǒ liǎng bēi shuǐ.
examples.en: Please give me two cups of water.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'two yuan' in spoken Chinese?
exercises.explanation: Before the measure word/unit 块, use 两 not 二. So 'two yuan' = 两块钱 (liǎng kuài qián).
¥15.80 = 十五块八毛. Since there are no 分, you can also just say 十五块八.

exercises.prompt: In a phone number, how is the digit 1 typically read?
exercises.explanation: In phone numbers and other digit-by-digit readings, 1 is read as 幺 (yāo) to avoid confusion with 七 (qī), which sounds similar to 一 (yī) over the phone.
